description: This figure presents the tensile properties of as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr austenitic low-density steels, where x equals 0, 3, 6, 9 wt.% Cr (denoted as 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, 9Cr respectively, with 4 curves corresponding to the four compositions in both subplots). Subplot (a) plots engineering stress (in MPa, 0-1000 range) against engineering strain (in %, 0-50 range). Subplot (b) presents true stress (in MPa, 0-1500 range, left y-axis) and strain hardening rate (in MPa, 1000-4000 range, right y-axis) as functions of true strain (0-0.4 range). Key trends observed: as Cr content increases, ultimate tensile strength increases (from ~900 MPa for 0Cr to ~1000 MPa for 9Cr) while elongation to fracture decreases (from ~49% for 0Cr to ~25% for 9Cr); the strain hardening rate at equivalent true strain rises significantly with higher Cr addition, and the 0Cr alloy shows the lowest and fastest-declining strain hardening rate during deformation. The figure supports that Cr addition improves the strength and strain hardening ability of the tested low-density steels but reduces their tensile ductility.
